title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Dylan Brooks, who studies sea life.
audio_script: Interviewer: My guest today is Dylan Brooks, who studies sea life around the British coast. Dylan, did you always want to be a marine biologist? || Guest: I loved the sea, but I wanted to be a journalist at first. Then I joined a school trip where we counted crabs and small fish in rock pools. [20]I was amazed that there was so much life in places I had walked past without noticing[/20]. After that I started reading about the coast and changed my plans. || Interviewer: What does your work involve on a normal day? || Guest: It depends on the weather. If the sea is calm, we go out by boat and take samples. But [21]on many days I spend hours entering information into a computer so we can compare results over time[/21]. People imagine exciting underwater work, and there is some of that, but careful records are just as important. || Interviewer: What do you enjoy most? || Guest: I enjoy diving, of course, but [22]the best part is finding small improvements in areas where people have worked hard to protect the sea[/22]. For example, if we see more young fish in a protected area, it suggests the rules are helping. Those moments make the slow work feel worthwhile. || Interviewer: What worries you about the coast? || Guest: Plastic rubbish is a big problem, but people know about it now. [23]I'm also worried by how much noise from boats can disturb animals that use sound to find food[/23]. You can't see noise floating on the water, so it is harder to explain, but it can affect sea life seriously. || Interviewer: You give talks in schools. What message do you want students to remember? || Guest: I tell them they don't need to live beside the sea to help it. [24]Simple choices, like using less plastic and not wasting water, can still make a difference[/24]. I also want them to be curious. If they visit a beach, they should look closely and ask what lives there. || Interviewer: What's next for your research? || Guest: This summer we're working with local fishermen. [25]We want to test new nets that allow very young fish to escape while keeping the adult fish[/25]. If it works, it could protect future fish numbers without stopping people earning a living from the sea. || Interviewer: Do you think the public is becoming more interested in the sea? || Guest: Definitely. Programmes on television have helped, but local projects are important too. When people join a beach survey or a clean-up, they begin to feel responsible for the place. I try not to make people feel guilty, because that often makes them switch off. Instead, I show them something surprising, like a tiny animal living under seaweed. Once they care about what is there, they are much more willing to protect it and to change small habits. I also work with volunteers who send photographs from beaches after storms. Their records help us notice changes quickly, and they prove that ordinary people can contribute to real scientific work.  It also helps scientists cover more coastline than we could manage alone.  That keeps the work connected to real places.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: My guest today is Dylan Brooks, who studies sea life around the British coast. Dylan, did you always want to be a marine biologist?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I loved the sea, but I wanted to be a journalist at first. Then I joined a school trip where we counted crabs and small fish in rock pools. I was amazed that there was so much life in places I had walked past without noticing. After that I started reading about the coast and changed my plans.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What does your work involve on a normal day?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: It depends on the weather. If the sea is calm, we go out by boat and take samples. But on many days I spend hours entering information into a computer so we can compare results over time. People imagine exciting underwater work, and there is some of that, but careful records are just as important.

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I enjoy diving, of course, but the best part is finding small improvements in areas where people have worked hard to protect the sea. For example, if we see more young fish in a protected area, it suggests the rules are helping. Those moments make the slow work feel worthwhile.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What worries you about the coast?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Plastic rubbish is a big problem, but people know about it now. I'm also worried by how much noise from boats can disturb animals that use sound to find food. You can't see noise floating on the water, so it is harder to explain, but it can affect sea life seriously.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: You give talks in schools. What message do you want students to remember?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I tell them they don't need to live beside the sea to help it. Simple choices, like using less plastic and not wasting water, can still make a difference. I also want them to be curious. If they visit a beach, they should look closely and ask what lives there.

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: This summer we're working with local fishermen. We want to test new nets that allow very young fish to escape while keeping the adult fish. If it works, it could protect future fish numbers without stopping people earning a living from the sea.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Do you think the public is becoming more interested in the sea?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Definitely. Programmes on television have helped, but local projects are important too. When people join a beach survey or a clean-up, they begin to feel responsible for the place. I try not to make people feel guilty, because that often makes them switch off. Instead, I show them something surprising, like a tiny animal living under seaweed. Once they care about what is there, they are much more willing to protect it and to change small habits. I also work with volunteers who send photographs from beaches after storms. Their records help us notice changes quickly, and they prove that ordinary people can contribute to real scientific work.  It also helps scientists cover more coastline than we could manage alone.  That keeps the work connected to real places.

questions.question: Why did Dylan become interested in marine biology?

options.A: He discovered unexpected sea life on a school trip.
options.B: He read a book by a famous scientist.
options.C: He wanted to write about environmental problems.

questions.explanation: Dylan says he was amazed by the life he saw in rock pools on a school trip.

questions.question: What does Dylan say about his normal work?

options.B: It includes a lot of computer work.
options.C: It is impossible in bad weather.

questions.explanation: He says he often spends hours entering information into a computer.

questions.question: What gives Dylan most satisfaction?

options.C: seeing signs that protection is working

questions.explanation: Dylan says the best part is finding improvements in protected areas.

questions.question: What environmental problem does Dylan mention?
questions.explanation: He says he is worried by noise from boats disturbing animals.

questions.question: What does Dylan tell students?

options.A: They should study science at school.
options.B: They can help the sea through everyday choices.
options.C: They need to visit the coast more often.

questions.explanation: He says simple choices such as using less plastic can make a difference.

questions.question: What will Dylan test this summer?

options.B: a method for counting young fish
options.C: nets that let young fish escape

questions.explanation: He says they want to test nets that allow very young fish to escape.
